metadata.title: One Mistake Took Down a 29-Yr-Old Dark Web Drug Lord

raw_text:
Video
One Mistake Took Down a 29-Yr-Old Dark Web Drug Lord

Transcript
Kind: captions Language: en [00:00] On Oct. 1, 2013, federal agents entered  a public library in San Francisco. [00:06] They arrested 29-year-old Ross Ulbricht. [00:08] Ulbricht ran the largest, most sophisticated  online market for illegal drugs in history. [00:14] He named it Silk Road, a reference to the ancient   [00:17] trade routes that connected China to  Europe beginning in the 2nd century BC. [00:22] Ulbricht hoped to create his  own modern-day marketplace.   [00:25] Except his would sell hardcore  drugs and other illegal goods. [00:29] Over the two years and ten  months that Silk Road operated,   [00:32] federal prosecutors say it processed  nearly $214 million in sales using Bitcoin. [00:38] The site operated on a hidden part of the internet called the dark web. [00:42] Prosecutors say a journal the FBI found on Ulbricht's computer [00:45] stated he wanted “to create a website where  people could buy anything anonymously,   [00:49] with no trail whatsoever that  could lead back to them.” [00:52] Unfortunately for Ulbricht, he did  leave a trail of digital breadcrumbs   [00:56] that would ultimately take him  down and his empire with it. [00:59] Ulbricht was born on March  27, 1984, in Austin, Texas. [01:04] He was a boy scout, attaining the highest rank  of eagle scout just like his dad had done. [01:08] He had a happy childhood. [01:10] Growing up an easy-going hipster but  serious student who scored 1460 on   [01:15] his SATs - within the 96th percentile - and got a   [01:18] full ride to the University of Texas  at Dallas* (NOT Austin), where he studied physics. [01:22] He then won another full  scholarship for a Master’s   [01:25] at Penn State in materials  science and engineering. [01:28] It was at Penn that he evolved  into a hardcore libertarian…a   [01:31] political philosophy that advocates individualism  and minimal state involvement in people’s lives. [01:36] He was a fan and follower of  libertarian economist Ludwig   [01:40] von Mises who opposed government  interference in the economy. [01:43] When then-presidential candidate Mitt Romney  asked what is America’s greatest challenge,   [01:48] Ulbricht responded like  this on his YouTube channel. [01:51] I think the most important thing is  getting us out of the United Nations. [01:55] Ulbricht wanted to create a world free  from institutional or governmental control. [02:00] That mindset led him to create  Silk Road in January 2011. [02:04] You couldn’t type in a regular web address to  get to Silk Road or use a regular web browser. [02:09] You needed software called Tor  that works as a web browser. [02:13] Tor was developed by the U.S. Navy as a way  of communicating privately over the internet. [02:18] It conceals the real IP address  of computers on the network to   [02:21] hide the identity of the user and it  can’t be traced by the government. [02:24] Silk Road’s address used a bunch of random  numbers and letters that ended with dot onion. [02:29] Ulbricht made the site by  teaching himself how to code. [02:32] When he needed more help he reached out  on a Bitcoin community forum, writing: [02:36] “I'm looking for the best and brightest  IT pro in the bitcoin community to be   [02:40] the lead developer in a venture  backed bitcoin startup company.” [02:44] Anyone interested was to contact him  via his email: rossulbricht@gmail.com [02:50] Making his email public would  later come back to haunt him. [02:54] He also got coding help from a buddy  of his from undergrad, Richard Bates. [02:58] Ulbricht had no choice but to eventually  tell his friend what he was up to.   [03:01] He also told his girlfriend Julia Vie. [03:04] One day, he showed her the psychedelic mushrooms   [03:06] he was growing and selling as a  starter product on his new website. [03:09] Silk Road would eventually be a  marketplace for all kinds of drugs:   [03:13] Weed. Cocaine. LSD. Ecstasy. Heroin. [03:17] This fit Ulbricht’s libertarian mindset. [03:19] He believed that whatever someone decided to put  in their body was their choice and no one else’s,   [03:24] least of all, the government’s. Ulbricht also believed everyone   [03:28] had the right to self-defense when guns  started appearing on Silk Road. However,   [03:32] he realized he didn’t need the controversy  and soon took weapons off the site. [03:36] After getting his business up and running – he  turned his attention to attracting customers. [03:40] He decided to write a post on a Magic  Mushrooms forum called the Shroomery,   [03:44] pretending to be someone who  happened to come across Silk Road. [03:47] He used the username “altoid”, posting:  “I'm thinking of buying off it,   [03:49] but wanted to see if anyone here had  heard of it and could recommend it.” [03:50] He included a link with instructions  on how to access Silk Road. [03:50] I came across this website called Silk Road.  It's a Tor hidden service that claims to   [03:50] allow you to buy and sell anything online  anonymously. I'm thinking of buying off it,   [03:51] but wanted to see if anyone here had  heard of it and could recommend it. [03:54] He did the same on a Bitcoin site in a  forum about buying and selling heroin,   [04:02] describing Silk Road as “an anonymous amazon.com” [04:06] It wasn’t long before buyers showed up. [04:08] To limit scams, there was a rating system  for sellers, similar to Amazon reviews.   [04:12] If a seller sold bad drugs and got a  poor rating, it would hurt their sales. [04:16] The drugs arrived by mail  with fake return addresses.   [04:20] They’d be slipped inside CD and DVD cases. [04:23] Some sellers got even more creative and  put them in little ripples of cardboard. [04:27] The packages had printed mailing  labels rather than handwritten ones   [04:30] to look like they came from a legitimate business. [04:33] Ironically, that backfired. The printed labels  actually attracted the suspicion of authorities. [04:39] In the summer of 2011, Department of Homeland  Security agent Jared Der-Yeghiayan learned of a   [04:44] small, neat package with a printed address going  through Chicago’s O’Hare International Airport. [04:49] It contained a single pink pill of  ecstasy which was also suspicious   [04:53] because usually, they shipped in bulk. [04:55] Soon, two or three packages began arriving,   [04:58] then 50, then up to 1000 a day! Many came from the  Netherlands which is a notorious source of drugs. [05:04] Agent Der-Yeghiayan visited an address where  one of the packages was to be delivered,   [05:08] chatted with the roommate of the buyer, who said  the drugs came from a site called Silk Road. [05:13] Der Yeghiayan had never heard of Silk Road before. [05:16] He did some digging online and  came across an article written   [05:18] by Gawker journalist Adrian Chen in June 2011. [05:22] Chen wrote: “Making small talk  with your pot dealer sucks.   [05:25] Buying cocaine can get you shot. What  if you could buy and sell drugs online   [05:29] like books or light bulbs? Now  you can: Welcome to Silk Road.” [05:34] The article attracted 3 million  views and put Silk Road on the map. [05:38] Not only did Silk Road soon attract the  attention of thousands of drug dealers and buyers   [05:43] but also politicians like senator Chuck Schumer  who called for the site to be shut down. [05:48] The U.S. government was concerned  but not only about drug sales. [05:52] As Nick Bilton detailed in  his book American Kingpin,   [05:55] Homeland Security agent Der-Yeghiayan feared that  a terrorist organization could enter the country   [06:00] and then buy something from  Silk Road to harm Americans. [06:03] He convinced the U.S. Attorney's  Office in Chicago to take on the case. [06:06] As Silk Road came into the spotlight,  Ulbricht’s college friend Richard who   [06:10] helped him with programming said  he urged him to shut it down. [06:13] Ulbricht and his girlfriend Julia broke  up soon after the launch of Silk Road.   [06:17] She said one of the reasons was because of the  insane pressure she felt to keep his secret. [06:22] Ulbricht lied to her and Richard, telling them   [06:25] he had sold the business to someone else  and no longer had anything to do with it. [06:29] He moved to Australia for a while  and lived with his sister in Sydney.  [06:32] Around this time, he was contacted through the   [06:34] site by a person going by  the name “Variety Jones”. [06:38] Variety Jones became his right-hand man and  someone Ulbricht described as a “real mentor”. [06:43] Neither knew the others’ true identity. [06:45] Variety Jones pointed out the gaping  holes in security on Silk Road. [06:49] Ulbricht decided to encrypt  all the files on his computer. [06:52] This is the actual laptop  he used to run Silk Road. [06:56] He put in a “kill switch” that would  automatically shut down his device   [06:59] by pressing a predetermined key in case  authorities rushed in at the last minute. [07:03] He also prepared an escape  plan if needed, including:   [07:06] destroy laptop, hard drive, find a place to  live on Craigslist for cash with a new identity. [07:12] Variety Jones came up with Ulbricht’s infamous  pseudonym on Silk Road: Dread Pirate Roberts [07:18] A reference to the fearsome captains  from the film The Princess Bride   [07:21] who passed the name on to a chosen successor. [07:24] In the same way, Ulbricht hoped to one day pass   [07:26] on the name Dread Pirate Roberts  to someone who might succeed him. [07:30] Variety Jones got him to see how  big Silk Road could grow to be. [07:35] Ulbricht wrote in a personal journal disclosed  by prosecutors: “Silk Road is going to become   [07:40] a phenomenon and at least one person will tell  me about it, unknowing that I was its creator.” [07:46] In two short years, Silk Road grew to more than  100,000 users with sales of nearly $214 million [07:57] The Feds were left scratching their heads as they   [07:59] still had no clue who was the  mastermind behind Silk Road. [08:03] Who was this Dread Pirates Roberts?  Who was the captain of the ship? [08:07] It became somewhat of a competition amongst the   [08:09] various government agencies to be the  one to identify Dread Pirate Roberts. [08:14] To better understand how Silk Road  operated, Department of Homeland   [08:17] Security agent Der-Yeghiayan posed as a  buyer and made 52 undercover purchases. [08:22] He also seized thousands of packages,   [08:24] linked certain sales back to their  source, and arrested several people. [08:28] His biggest get came when he tracked  down a Dread Pirate Roberts’ employee,   [08:32] a moderator on Silk Road’s  user forums called “cirrus”. [08:36] He forced her to hand over her account  and then, he pretended to be her. [08:40] Der-Yeghiayan posing as cirrus got assignments  directly from Dread Pirate Roberts. [08:44] He was not the only federal agent  chatting with the boss of Silk Road. [08:48] DEA agent Carl Force was part of a task force  in Baltimore that was also investigating. [08:54] Force used the username “Nob” and posed as a drug  dealer originally from the Dominican Republic   [08:59] who smuggled millions of dollars worth of  cocaine and heroin into the U.S. every year. [09:04] He was on friendly terms with Dread Pirate  Roberts, who had no idea he was speaking with   [09:08] a DEA agent. An agent who, in a twist  in the tale, turned out to be corrupt. [09:14] Force convinced Dread Pirate Roberts to  pay him $50,000 in Bitcoin by claiming   [09:19] he had “insider” information  from a government employee. [09:22] When Force reported the conversation to  the DEA, he claimed he never received   [09:26] any payment when in fact, he funneled  the Bitcoin into a personal account. [09:30] And believe it or not, a SECOND  agent who worked on the same   [09:33] Baltimore task force was also stealing,  Security Service agent Shaun Bridges. [09:38] When Silk Road customer support rep Curtis  Clark Green was arrested at his home with a kilo   [09:43] of coke, Bridges used Green’s admin access to  steal 20,000 Bitcoin from other user accounts.   [09:49] That was worth $350,000 dollars and swelled  to $820,000 by the time Bridges liquidated it. [09:49] Dread Pirate Roberts thought Green  to be responsible for the theft.   [09:52] He wanted to rough him up and got  egged on by his mentor Variety Jones. [09:56] Dread Pirate Roberts knew Green’s real  identity because as a condition for being   [10:00] on Silk Road’s payroll, staff had  to hand over their government ID. [10:03] So Dread Pirate Roberts turned to Nob (aka DEA  agent Carl Force) to beat up Green. Nob agreed. [10:10] However, Dread Pirate Roberts then  changed his mind and messaged:   [10:14] "Can you change the order to  execute rather than torture?" [10:18] Dread Pirate Roberts said he had “never  killed a man or had one killed before,   [10:22] but it is the right move in this case.” [10:24] He didn’t want to risk Green giving  up information to the authorities as   [10:28] he knew Green had been arrested  when he searched him up online. [10:31] Nob agreed to do the job for $80,000 in Bitcoin. [10:34] Ulbricht later received photos of a dead Green. [10:37] Except he wasn’t really dead. [10:39] Agent Force staged Green’s death  complete with photos of him on the   [10:43] floor covered in Campbell’s Chicken & Stars soup. [10:46] The Dread Pirate Roberts is said to have ordered  hits on five others whom he felt threatened by. [10:51] Silk Road had been prey for  blackmailers and extortionists. [10:54] However, there was no proof  that anyone was ever killed.   [10:58] Ulbricht was never charged with murder for hire. [11:01] The government agencies were still nowhere closer  to figuring out who was Dread Pirate Roberts. [11:06] The DEA enlisted the help of the  FBI’s Cyber Crime unit in New York   [11:10] as it had more technological know-how  and experience with the dark web. [11:14] FBI special agent Chris Tarbell knew that  in order to catch Dread Pirate Roberts,   [11:19] they had to wait for them to make a mistake. [11:21] And according to the FBI, the  Dread Pirate Roberts did finally   [11:24] slip up about a year after the  agency started investigating. [11:28] In May 2013, investigators noticed  coding errors - vulnerabilities on   [11:33] the Silk Road website that leaked IP addresses. [11:36] As a result, they discovered the Silk Road  servers were housed in a data center in Iceland. [11:40] By the way, Ulbricht’s defense  team doesn’t buy this explanation.   [11:44] They believe the NSA spied illegally and  tipped off the FBI to the servers’ location. [11:49] Agent Tarbell flew to Reykjavik  where Icelandic authorities gave   [11:52] him a drive with information from the servers. [11:55] The FBI had access to a treasure trove of  data: They could see the number of transactions   [11:59] processed. Who logged in and out. And crucially, Tarbell and his   [12:03] team had identified that the master  computer Silk Road servers talked to,   [12:06] the one Dread Pirate Roberts used to  log in to Silk Road, was named “Frosty”. [12:11] And Dread Pirate Roberts was logging in with  an encryption key that ended with frosty@frosty [12:16] They could also tell Dread Pirate  Roberts recently used internet from   [12:19] a San Francisco cafe to log in  to a Silk Road server via a VPN. [12:24] By the spring of 2012, Ulbright had  returned home from Australia and   [12:28] eventually moved to San Francisco  to live with a childhood friend. [12:31] So now the FBI had the name of the computer  and could focus their search on San Francisco. [12:36] But still, no idea of Dread  Pirate Roberts’ real identity. [12:40] That piece of the puzzle would be filled in   [12:42] by Gary Alford who worked for the  IRS…the Internal Revenue Service. [12:46] As a tax investigator, he was assigned  to follow the money but instead,   [12:50] discovered the identity of Dread Pirate Roberts. [12:53] Alford figured that whoever  started Silk Road would have   [12:56] had to drum up interest in it long  before that Gawker article came out. [13:00] So he decided to do Google searches  for Silk Road prior to June 2011. [13:04] That’s when he stumbled on Ulbricht’s online posts   [13:06] where he pretended to be someone who  happened to come across Silk Road. [13:10] Alford noted the username for the posts: “altoid”. [13:13] He also noticed another post  where “altoid” asked for IT   [13:16] help on the Bitcoin forum which included  his personal email: rossulbricht@gmail.com [13:22] Someone with the username Altoid also posted on  Stack Overflow with a question related to Tor. [13:28] Soon after, that user changed  the alias from Altoid to Frosty. [13:33] Alford didn’t know the significance  of the name Frosty at the time. [13:37] But what he did have was  a real name to track down. [13:40] He googled Ross Ulbricht and came across the  LinkedIn profile of a young man with rather   [13:45] cryptic life goals, quote: “The most  widespread and systemic use of force   [13:49] is amongst institutions and governments,  so this is my current point of effort.” [13:54] Alford asked himself: Could Ross  Ulbricht be the brains behind Silk Road? [13:59] Then came another piece of the puzzle. [14:01] In July 2013, U.S. Customs and Border  Protection officers intercepted nine   [14:06] fake IDs coming into the U.S. from Canada. [14:09] Homeland security agents decided to  pay a visit to the intended recipient. [14:13] They showed up at 2260 15th  Avenue in San Francisco,   [14:16] the address where Ulbricht had been staying. [14:18] He had moved out of the place he  shared with his friend and into   [14:21] a sublet he found on Craigslist  where he paid his rent in cash. [14:25] He took every precaution,  including adopting a fake identity. [14:28] His roommates knew him as Joshua Terrey  though he kept much of his backstory the same. [14:33] He said he was from Texas. Worked in IT  which is why he was always on his computer. [14:37] And had recently returned  home from Australia which   [14:40] made not having a cell phone less suspicious. [14:43] “Joshua” kept to himself and  ate steak dinners for one. [14:46] When the agents questioned Ulbricht, they  observed that he became “visibly nervous”.   [14:51] He didn’t admit to purchasing the fake IDs  - let alone that he bought them so he could   [14:55] rent additional servers to deal with Silk  Road’s growth as well as security issues. [14:59] Prosecutors later claimed he  prepared for a life on the run   [15:02] including seeking citizenship  on the island of Dominica. [15:05] Ulbricht volunteered to the agents who  showed up at his door that “hypothetically”   [15:09] anyone could purchase anything they  wanted on a site called Silk Road. [15:13] The agents had never heard of Silk Road and were  apparently unaware of any federal investigations. [15:18] But they did document the incident. [15:21] So when IRS agent Gary Alford ran another search  on Ulbricht, he found that Homeland Security   [15:26] file on the fake IDs - which cemented  his belief that they had the right guy. [15:30] The pieces of the puzzle finally came  together when Alford got on the phone   [15:34] with the other agencies to see what they all had.   [15:36] Alford mentioned the username  “Frosty” that he had found on a forum. [15:40] That floored FBI agent Tarbell  - who knew Frosty to be the   [15:43] name of the computer Dread Pirate Roberts used. [15:46] Bingo. They knew Ross Ulbricht  was Dread Pirate Roberts. [15:51] In their pursuit of evidence, federal  agents followed him everywhere. [15:54] They also had a subpoena for Gmail and noticed  that whenever Dread Pirate Roberts logged into   [15:58] Silk Road, Ulbricht was also signed into  his Google account. And whenever Dread   [16:03] Pirate Roberts logged out, Ulbricht  also logged out of his Google account. [16:07] But they knew this wouldn’t be enough to nail him. [16:09] They needed concrete evidence. [16:11] They had to catch Ulbright  while his laptop was open   [16:14] and he was logged in on Silk  Road as Dread Pirate Roberts. [16:18] An unlocked computer was also necessary  so agents could access his files. [16:22] This would be difficult to pull off. [16:24] Authorities knew their best bet was to catch  him off guard and arrest him in a public place. [16:29] Homeland Security agent Jared Der-Yeghiayan was  still pretending to be Silk Road staff member   [16:33] cirrus and continued to maintain  contact with Dread Pirate Roberts. [16:37] This communication was crucial  to the plan they hatched. [16:40] On October 1st, 2013 at 3:08 pm local time,  Der-Yeghiayan saw Dread Pirate Roberts log on. [16:48] Ulbricht was sitting in the  science fiction section of the   [16:51] San Francisco public library’s Glen Park branch. [16:54] Der-Yeghiayan started up a conversation with  Dread Pirate Roberts while posing as Cirrus. [16:59] hi are you there  [17:01] hey how are you doing?  [17:04] i'm ok, you? good. can you check   [17:08] out on one of the flagged messages for me? The flagged messages were in the main   [17:12] administrative section that only  Dread Pirate Roberts could access. [17:16] Dread Pirate Roberts responded: sure  [17:19] let me log in ok  [17:22] you did bitcoin exchange before  you started working for me, right?  [17:25] Ulbricht asked this for security reasons because  only the real cirrus would know the answer. [17:32] Luckily for authorities, the real Cirrus had  told them how to answer the security question. [17:36] Agent Der-Yeghiayan hoped he  recalled correctly when he typed: [17:40] yes, but just for a little bit [17:42] It was a nerve-racking moment.  [17:45] not any more than that no. I stopped because of reporting requirements  [17:51] damn regulators eh? ok, which post?  [17:55] Der-Yeghiayan knew Ulbricht was  now looking at an admin screen.   [17:58] He gave the signal to the other agents  positioned in the library to make a move. [18:02] A commotion soon broke out near Ulbricht. [18:05] A woman yelled, “F–k you” as the man  appeared like he was about to punch her. [18:09] When Ulbricht turned around to see the fight, an  agent swooped in and grabbed his opened laptop. [18:14] At that point, agent Der-Yeghiayan said  Ulbricht “lunges towards the laptop.   [18:18] And another agent walks  behind him - bear hugs him…” [18:21] Ulbricht was arrested and taken away. [18:23] The man and woman who were arguing were in fact  undercover agents who created a distraction. [18:29] Ulbricht’s family and friends were  shocked. How could the kind, fun,   [18:34] loving man they knew be the same person who ran  a global drug bazaar? They couldn’t believe it. [18:39] Ulbricht was indicted on seven charges: including  distributing narcotics by means of the internet   [18:45] and engaging in a continuing criminal enterprise. [18:48] He pleaded not guilty to all charges. [18:51] The trial began on January 13,  2015 and lasted three weeks. [18:56] Many of Ulbricht’s supporters showed up…believing   [18:58] a guilty verdict would be  a miscarriage of justice. [19:01] In court, Ulbricht’s defense  attorney argued he wasn’t Dread   [19:04] Pirate Roberts - that he had given away  the handle and Silk Road to someone else. [19:08] But the jury didn’t buy it. [19:10] On February 5, 2015, a federal jury found  Ross Ulbricht guilty on all seven counts. [19:17] Before sentencing, Ulbricht wrote a letter to  the judge, begging for a more lenient sentence: [19:22] “I've had my youth, and I know you must take away  my middle years, but please leave me my old age.   [19:28] Please leave a small light at the end of the  tunnel, an excuse to stay healthy, an excuse to   [19:34] dream of better days ahead, and a chance to redeem  myself in the free world before I meet my maker.” [19:42] On May 29, 2015, U.S. District Judge  Katherine B. Forrest delivered her sentence: [19:48] "You were captain of the ship, as the Dread  Pirate Roberts, and you made your own laws and   [19:53] you enforced those laws in the manner that you saw  fit. So, it wasn't a world without restriction. It   [19:58] wasn't a world of ultimate freedom. It was a world  of laws that you created, they were your laws.   [20:05] It is fictional to think of Silk  Road as some place of freedom." [20:08] "No drug dealer from the Bronx selling meth  or heroin or crack has ever made these kinds   [20:13] of arguments to the Court. It is a privileged  argument, it is an argument from one of privilege.   [20:19] You are no better a person than any  other drug dealer and your education   [20:23] does not give you a special place of  privilege in our criminal justice system.   [20:27] It makes it less explicable  why you did what you did." [20:30] "There is no reason to make a choice between these  two people that I see that are on display - the   [20:35] Ulbricht who is the leader of the criminal  enterprise and the Ulbricht who is known and   [20:39] loved. What is clear is that people are very,  very complex and you are one of them. They are   [20:45] made up of many different qualities and many  characteristics with no one quality defining   [20:50] them. And there is good in Mr. Ulbricht,  I have no doubt, but there is also bad,   [20:55] and what you did in connection with Silk Road  was terribly destructive to our social fabric. [21:01] Mr. Ulbricht, it is my judgment delivered  here, now, on behalf of our country,   [21:06] that on Counts Two and Four you are sentenced to  a period of life imprisonment to run concurrently;   [21:12] on Count Five you are sentenced to five  years’ imprisonment to run concurrently;   [21:17] on Count Six, you are sentenced to 15  years’ imprisonment also concurrent;   [21:21] and for money laundering in Count Seven, you  are sentenced to 20 years, also concurrent.   [21:26] In the federal system there is no parole  and you shall serve your life in prison. [21:34] This was far beyond what the  prosecution had even asked for.  [21:38] Ulbricht had hoped Silk Road would become  such a success that he would be famous. [21:43] According to a journal entry read  by the prosecutor to the jury,   [21:46] Ulbricht wrote: “I imagine that someday  I may have a story written about my life   [21:50] and it would be good to have  a detailed account of it.” [21:53] His dream came true…but perhaps  not in the way he had imagined. [21:58] The FBI later identified  Ulbricht’s mentor, Variety Jones,   [22:02] as Roger Thomas Clark, a Canadian arrested  in Thailand in 2015. In 2020, Clark pled   [22:08] guilty in a Manhattan court to conspiring to  distribute massive quantities of narcotics. [22:13] As for the two corrupt officers  investigating Silk Road. [22:16] Former DEA agent Carl Force was sentenced  to 6 ½ years in prison for extortion,   [22:21] money laundering, and obstruction of justice. [22:24] Former Secret Service agent Shaun Bridges  was sentenced to nearly 6 years in prison   [22:28] on charges of money laundering and obstruction  of justice. He was later sentenced to an   [22:33] additional two years for ANOTHER theft  of Bitcoin from the U.S. government. [22:39] Thanks for watching. For Newsthink, I’m Cindy Pom.

sections.Transcript: Kind: captions Language: en [00:00] On Oct. 1, 2013, federal agents entered  a public library in San Francisco. [00:06] They arrested 29-year-old Ross Ulbricht. [00:08] Ulbricht ran the largest, most sophisticated  online market for illegal drugs in history. [00:14] He named it Silk Road, a reference to the ancient   [00:17] trade routes that connected China to  Europe beginning in the 2nd century BC. [00:22] Ulbricht hoped to create his  own modern-day marketplace.   [00:25] Except his would sell hardcore  drugs and other illegal goods. [00:29] Over the two years and ten  months that Silk Road operated,   [00:32] federal prosecutors say it processed  nearly $214 million in sales using Bitcoin. [00:38] The site operated on a hidden part of the internet called the dark web. [00:42] Prosecutors say  a journal the FBI found on Ulbricht's computer [00:45] stated he wanted “to create a website where  people could buy anything anonymously,   [00:49] with no trail whatsoever that  could lead back to them.” [00:52] Unfortunately for Ulbricht, he did  leave a trail of digital breadcrumbs   [00:56] that would ultimately take him  down and his empire with it. [00:59] Ulbricht was born on March  27, 1984, in Austin, Texas. [01:04] He was a boy scout, attaining the highest rank  of eagle scout just like his dad had done. [01:08] He had a happy childhood. [01:10] Growing up an easy-going hipster but  serious student who scored 1460 on   [01:15] his SATs - within the 96th percentile - and got a   [01:18] full ride to the University of Texas  at Dallas* (NOT Austin), where he studied physics. [01:22] He then won another full  scholarship for a Master’s   [01:25] at Penn State in materials  science and engineering. [01:28] It was at Penn that he evolved  into a hardcore libertarian…a   [01:31] political philosophy that advocates individualism  and minimal state involvement in people’s lives. [01:36] He was a fan and follower of  libertarian economist Ludwig   [01:40] von Mises who opposed government  interference in the economy. [01:43] When then-presidential candidate Mitt Romney  asked what is America’s greatest challenge,   [01:48] Ulbricht responded like  this on his YouTube channel. [01:51] I think the most important thing is  getting us out of the United Nations. [01:55] Ulbricht wanted to create a world free  from institutional or governmental control. [02:00] That mindset led him to create  Silk Road in January 2011. [02:04] You couldn’t type in a regular web address to  get to Silk Road or use a regular web browser. [02:09] You needed software called Tor  that works as a web browser. [02:13] Tor was developed by the U.S. Navy as a way  of communicating privately over the internet. [02:18] It conceals the real IP address  of computers on the network to   [02:21] hide the identity of the user and it  can’t be traced by the government. [02:24] Silk Road’s address used a bunch of random  numbers and letters that ended with dot onion. [02:29] Ulbricht made the site by  teaching himself how to code. [02:32] When he needed more help he reached out  on a Bitcoin community forum, writing: [02:36] “I'm looking for the best and brightest  IT pro in the bitcoin community to be   [02:40] the lead developer in a venture  backed bitcoin startup company.” [02:44] Anyone interested was to contact him  via his email: rossulbricht@gmail.com [02:50] Making his email public would  later come back to haunt him. [02:54] He also got coding help from a buddy  of his from undergrad, Richard Bates. [02:58] Ulbricht had no choice but to eventually  tell his friend what he was up to.   [03:01] He also told his girlfriend Julia Vie. [03:04] One day, he showed her the psychedelic mushrooms   [03:06] he was growing and selling as a  starter product on his new website. [03:09] Silk Road would eventually be a  marketplace for all kinds of drugs:   [03:13] Weed. Cocaine. LSD. Ecstasy. Heroin. [03:17] This fit Ulbricht’s libertarian mindset. [03:19] He believed that whatever someone decided to put  in their body was their choice and no one else’s,   [03:24] least of all, the government’s. Ulbricht also believed everyone   [03:28] had the right to self-defense when guns  started appearing on Silk Road. However,   [03:32] he realized he didn’t need the controversy  and soon took weapons off the site. [03:36] After getting his business up and running – he  turned his attention to attracting customers. [03:40] He decided to write a post on a Magic  Mushrooms forum called the Shroomery,   [03:44] pretending to be someone who  happened to come across Silk Road. [03:47] He used the username “altoid”, posting:  “I'm thinking of buying off it,   [03:49] but wanted to see if anyone here had  heard of it and could recommend it.” [03:50] He included a link with instructions  on how to access Silk Road. [03:50] I came across this website called Silk Road.  It's a Tor hidden service that claims to   [03:50] allow you to buy and sell anything online  anonymously. I'm thinking of buying off it,   [03:51] but wanted to see if anyone here had  heard of it and could recommend it. [03:54] He did the same on a Bitcoin site in a  forum about buying and selling heroin,   [04:02] describing Silk Road as “an anonymous amazon.com” [04:06] It wasn’t long before buyers showed up. [04:08] To limit scams, there was a rating system  for sellers, similar to Amazon reviews.   [04:12] If a seller sold bad drugs and got a  poor rating, it would hurt their sales. [04:16] The drugs arrived by mail  with fake return addresses.   [04:20] They’d be slipped inside CD and DVD cases. [04:23] Some sellers got even more creative and  put them in little ripples of cardboard. [04:27] The packages had printed mailing  labels rather than handwritten ones   [04:30] to look like they came from a legitimate business. [04:33] Ironically, that backfired. The printed labels  actually attracted the suspicion of authorities. [04:39] In the summer of 2011, Department of Homeland  Security agent Jared Der-Yeghiayan learned of a   [04:44] small, neat package with a printed address going  through Chicago’s O’Hare International Airport. [04:49] It contained a single pink pill of  ecstasy which was also suspicious   [04:53] because usually, they shipped in bulk. [04:55] Soon, two or three packages began arriving,   [04:58] then 50, then up to 1000 a day! Many came from the  Netherlands which is a notorious source of drugs. [05:04] Agent Der-Yeghiayan visited an address where  one of the packages was to be delivered,   [05:08] chatted with the roommate of the buyer, who said  the drugs came from a site called Silk Road. [05:13] Der Yeghiayan had never heard of Silk Road before. [05:16] He did some digging online and  came across an article written   [05:18] by Gawker journalist Adrian Chen in June 2011. [05:22] Chen wrote: “Making small talk  with your pot dealer sucks.   [05:25] Buying cocaine can get you shot. What  if you could buy and sell drugs online   [05:29] like books or light bulbs? Now  you can: Welcome to Silk Road.” [05:34] The article attracted 3 million  views and put Silk Road on the map. [05:38] Not only did Silk Road soon attract the  attention of thousands of drug dealers and buyers   [05:43] but also politicians like senator Chuck Schumer  who called for the site to be shut down. [05:48] The U.S. government was concerned  but not only about drug sales. [05:52] As Nick Bilton detailed in  his book American Kingpin,   [05:55] Homeland Security agent Der-Yeghiayan feared that  a terrorist organization could enter the country   [06:00] and then buy something from  Silk Road to harm Americans. [06:03] He convinced the U.S. Attorney's  Office in Chicago to take on the case. [06:06] As Silk Road came into the spotlight,  Ulbricht’s college friend Richard who   [06:10] helped him with programming said  he urged him to shut it down. [06:13] Ulbricht and his girlfriend Julia broke  up soon after the launch of Silk Road.   [06:17] She said one of the reasons was because of the  insane pressure she felt to keep his secret. [06:22] Ulbricht lied to her and Richard, telling them   [06:25] he had sold the business to someone else  and no longer had anything to do with it. [06:29] He moved to Australia for a while  and lived with his sister in Sydney.  [06:32] Around this time, he was contacted through the   [06:34] site by a person going by  the name “Variety Jones”. [06:38] Variety Jones became his right-hand man and  someone Ulbricht described as a “real mentor”. [06:43] Neither knew the others’ true identity. [06:45] Variety Jones pointed out the gaping  holes in security on Silk Road. [06:49] Ulbricht decided to encrypt  all the files on his computer. [06:52] This is the actual laptop  he used to run Silk Road. [06:56] He put in a “kill switch” that would  automatically shut down his device   [06:59] by pressing a predetermined key in case  authorities rushed in at the last minute. [07:03] He also prepared an escape  plan if needed, including:   [07:06] destroy laptop, hard drive, find a place to  live on Craigslist for cash with a new identity. [07:12] Variety Jones came up with Ulbricht’s infamous  pseudonym on Silk Road: Dread Pirate Roberts [07:18] A reference to the fearsome captains  from the film The Princess Bride   [07:21] who passed the name on to a chosen successor. [07:24] In the same way, Ulbricht hoped to one day pass   [07:26] on the name Dread Pirate Roberts  to someone who might succeed him. [07:30] Variety Jones got him to see how  big Silk Road could grow to be. [07:35] Ulbricht wrote in a personal journal disclosed  by prosecutors: “Silk Road is going to become   [07:40] a phenomenon and at least one person will tell  me about it, unknowing that I was its creator.” [07:46] In two short years, Silk Road grew to more than  100,000 users with sales of nearly $214 million [07:57] The Feds were left scratching their heads as they   [07:59] still had no clue who was the  mastermind behind Silk Road. [08:03] Who was this Dread Pirates Roberts?  Who was the captain of the ship? [08:07] It became somewhat of a competition amongst the   [08:09] various government agencies to be the  one to identify Dread Pirate Roberts. [08:14] To better understand how Silk Road  operated, Department of Homeland   [08:17] Security agent Der-Yeghiayan posed as a  buyer and made 52 undercover purchases. [08:22] He also seized thousands of packages,   [08:24] linked certain sales back to their  source, and arrested several people. [08:28] His biggest get came when he tracked  down a Dread Pirate Roberts’ employee,   [08:32] a moderator on Silk Road’s  user forums called “cirrus”. [08:36] He forced her to hand over her account  and then, he pretended to be her. [08:40] Der-Yeghiayan posing as cirrus got assignments  directly from Dread Pirate Roberts. [08:44] He was not the only federal agent  chatting with the boss of Silk Road. [08:48] DEA agent Carl Force was part of a task force  in Baltimore that was also investigating. [08:54] Force used the username “Nob” and posed as a drug  dealer originally from the Dominican Republic   [08:59] who smuggled millions of dollars worth of  cocaine and heroin into the U.S. every year. [09:04] He was on friendly terms with Dread Pirate  Roberts, who had no idea he was speaking with   [09:08] a DEA agent. An agent who, in a twist  in the tale, turned out to be corrupt. [09:14] Force convinced Dread Pirate Roberts to  pay him $50,000 in Bitcoin by claiming   [09:19] he had “insider” information  from a government employee. [09:22] When Force reported the conversation to  the DEA, he claimed he never received   [09:26] any payment when in fact, he funneled  the Bitcoin into a personal account. [09:30] And believe it or not, a SECOND  agent who worked on the same   [09:33] Baltimore task force was also stealing,  Security Service agent Shaun Bridges. [09:38] When Silk Road customer support rep Curtis  Clark Green was arrested at his home with a kilo   [09:43] of coke, Bridges used Green’s admin access to  steal 20,000 Bitcoin from other user accounts.   [09:49] That was worth $350,000 dollars and swelled  to $820,000 by the time Bridges liquidated it. [09:49] Dread Pirate Roberts thought Green  to be responsible for the theft.   [09:52] He wanted to rough him up and got  egged on by his mentor Variety Jones. [09:56] Dread Pirate Roberts knew Green’s real  identity because as a condition for being   [10:00] on Silk Road’s payroll, staff had  to hand over their government ID. [10:03] So Dread Pirate Roberts turned to Nob (aka DEA  agent Carl Force) to beat up Green. Nob agreed. [10:10] However, Dread Pirate Roberts then  changed his mind and messaged:   [10:14] "Can you change the order to  execute rather than torture?" [10:18] Dread Pirate Roberts said he had “never  killed a man or had one killed before,   [10:22] but it is the right move in this case.” [10:24] He didn’t want to risk Green giving  up information to the authorities as   [10:28] he knew Green had been arrested  when he searched him up online. [10:31] Nob agreed to do the job for $80,000 in Bitcoin. [10:34] Ulbricht later received photos of a dead Green. [10:37] Except he wasn’t really dead. [10:39] Agent Force staged Green’s death  complete with photos of him on the   [10:43] floor covered in Campbell’s Chicken & Stars soup. [10:46] The Dread Pirate Roberts is said to have ordered  hits on five others whom he felt threatened by. [10:51] Silk Road had been prey for  blackmailers and extortionists. [10:54] However, there was no proof  that anyone was ever killed.   [10:58] Ulbricht was never charged with murder for hire. [11:01] The government agencies were still nowhere closer  to figuring out who was Dread Pirate Roberts. [11:06] The DEA enlisted the help of the  FBI’s Cyber Crime unit in New York   [11:10] as it had more technological know-how  and experience with the dark web. [11:14] FBI special agent Chris Tarbell knew that  in order to catch Dread Pirate Roberts,   [11:19] they had to wait for them to make a mistake. [11:21] And according to the FBI, the  Dread Pirate Roberts did finally   [11:24] slip up about a year after the  agency started investigating. [11:28] In May 2013, investigators noticed  coding errors - vulnerabilities on   [11:33] the Silk Road website that leaked IP addresses. [11:36] As a result, they discovered the Silk Road  servers were housed in a data center in Iceland. [11:40] By the way, Ulbricht’s defense  team doesn’t buy this explanation.   [11:44] They believe the NSA spied illegally and  tipped off the FBI to the servers’ location. [11:49] Agent Tarbell flew to Reykjavik  where Icelandic authorities gave   [11:52] him a drive with information from the servers. [11:55] The FBI had access to a treasure trove of  data: They could see the number of transactions   [11:59] processed. Who logged in and out. And crucially, Tarbell and his   [12:03] team had identified that the master  computer Silk Road servers talked to,   [12:06] the one Dread Pirate Roberts used to  log in to Silk Road, was named “Frosty”. [12:11] And Dread Pirate Roberts was logging in with  an encryption key that ended with frosty@frosty [12:16] They could also tell Dread Pirate  Roberts recently used internet from   [12:19] a San Francisco cafe to log in  to a Silk Road server via a VPN. [12:24] By the spring of 2012, Ulbright had  returned home from Australia and   [12:28] eventually moved to San Francisco  to live with a childhood friend. [12:31] So now the FBI had the name of the computer  and could focus their search on San Francisco. [12:36] But still, no idea of Dread  Pirate Roberts’ real identity. [12:40] That piece of the puzzle would be filled in   [12:42] by Gary Alford who worked for the  IRS…the Internal Revenue Service. [12:46] As a tax investigator, he was assigned  to follow the money but instead,   [12:50] discovered the identity of Dread Pirate Roberts. [12:53] Alford figured that whoever  started Silk Road would have   [12:56] had to drum up interest in it long  before that Gawker article came out. [13:00] So he decided to do Google searches  for Silk Road prior to June 2011. [13:04] That’s when he stumbled on Ulbricht’s online posts   [13:06] where he pretended to be someone who  happened to come across Silk Road. [13:10] Alford noted the username for the posts: “altoid”. [13:13] He also noticed another post  where “altoid” asked for IT   [13:16] help on the Bitcoin forum which included  his personal email: rossulbricht@gmail.com [13:22] Someone with the username Altoid also posted on  Stack Overflow with a question related to Tor. [13:28] Soon after, that user changed  the alias from Altoid to Frosty. [13:33] Alford didn’t know the significance  of the name Frosty at the time. [13:37] But what he did have was  a real name to track down. [13:40] He googled Ross Ulbricht and came across the  LinkedIn profile of a young man with rather   [13:45] cryptic life goals, quote: “The most  widespread and systemic use of force   [13:49] is amongst institutions and governments,  so this is my current point of effort.” [13:54] Alford asked himself: Could Ross  Ulbricht be the brains behind Silk Road? [13:59] Then came another piece of the puzzle. [14:01] In July 2013, U.S. Customs and Border  Protection officers intercepted nine   [14:06] fake IDs coming into the U.S. from Canada. [14:09] Homeland security agents decided to  pay a visit to the intended recipient. [14:13] They showed up at 2260 15th  Avenue in San Francisco,   [14:16] the address where Ulbricht had been staying. [14:18] He had moved out of the place he  shared with his friend and into   [14:21] a sublet he found on Craigslist  where he paid his rent in cash. [14:25] He took every precaution,  including adopting a fake identity. [14:28] His roommates knew him as Joshua Terrey  though he kept much of his backstory the same. [14:33] He said he was from Texas. Worked in IT  which is why he was always on his computer. [14:37] And had recently returned  home from Australia which   [14:40] made not having a cell phone less suspicious. [14:43] “Joshua” kept to himself and  ate steak dinners for one. [14:46] When the agents questioned Ulbricht, they  observed that he became “visibly nervous”.   [14:51] He didn’t admit to purchasing the fake IDs  - let alone that he bought them so he could   [14:55] rent additional servers to deal with Silk  Road’s growth as well as security issues. [14:59] Prosecutors later claimed he  prepared for a life on the run   [15:02] including seeking citizenship  on the island of Dominica. [15:05] Ulbricht volunteered to the agents who  showed up at his door that “hypothetically”   [15:09] anyone could purchase anything they  wanted on a site called Silk Road. [15:40] That floored FBI agent Tarbell  - who knew Frosty to be the   [15:43] name of the computer Dread Pirate Roberts used. [15:46] Bingo. They knew Ross Ulbricht  was Dread Pirate Roberts. [15:51] In their pursuit of evidence, federal  agents followed him everywhere. And whenever Dread   [16:03] Pirate Roberts logged out, Ulbricht  also logged out of his Google account. [16:07] But they knew this wouldn’t be enough to nail him. [16:09] They needed concrete evidence. [16:11] They had to catch Ulbright  while his laptop was open   [16:14] and he was logged in on Silk  Road as Dread Pirate Roberts. [16:18] An unlocked computer was also necessary  so agents could access his files. [16:22] This would be difficult to pull off. [16:24] Authorities knew their best bet was to catch  him off guard and arrest him in a public place. The flagged messages were in the main   [17:12] administrative section that only  Dread Pirate Roberts could access. [17:16] Dread Pirate Roberts responded: sure  [17:19] let me log in ok  [17:22] you did bitcoin exchange before  you started working for me, right?  [17:25] Ulbricht asked this for security reasons because  only the real cirrus would know the answer. I stopped because of reporting requirements  [17:51] damn regulators eh? ok, which post?  [17:55] Der-Yeghiayan knew Ulbricht was  now looking at an admin screen.   [17:58] He gave the signal to the other agents  positioned in the library to make a move. [18:23] The man and woman who were arguing were in fact  undercover agents who created a distraction. [18:29] Ulbricht’s family and friends were  shocked. How could the kind, fun,   [18:34] loving man they knew be the same person who ran  a global drug bazaar? They couldn’t believe it. [18:39] Ulbricht was indicted on seven charges: including  distributing narcotics by means of the internet   [18:45] and engaging in a continuing criminal enterprise. [18:48] He pleaded not guilty to all charges. [18:51] The trial began on January 13,  2015 and lasted three weeks. [18:56] Many of Ulbricht’s supporters showed up…believing   [18:58] a guilty verdict would be  a miscarriage of justice. Forrest delivered her sentence: [19:48] "You were captain of the ship, as the Dread  Pirate Roberts, and you made your own laws and   [19:53] you enforced those laws in the manner that you saw  fit. So, it wasn't a world without restriction. It   [19:58] wasn't a world of ultimate freedom. It was a world  of laws that you created, they were your laws.   [20:05] It is fictional to think of Silk  Road as some place of freedom." [20:08] "No drug dealer from the Bronx selling meth  or heroin or crack has ever made these kinds   [20:13] of arguments to the Court. It is a privileged  argument, it is an argument from one of privilege.   [20:19] You are no better a person than any  other drug dealer and your education   [20:23] does not give you a special place of  privilege in our criminal justice system.   [20:27] It makes it less explicable  why you did what you did." [20:30] "There is no reason to make a choice between these  two people that I see that are on display - the   [20:35] Ulbricht who is the leader of the criminal  enterprise and the Ulbricht who is known and   [20:39] loved. What is clear is that people are very,  very complex and you are one of them. [22:24] Former Secret Service agent Shaun Bridges  was sentenced to nearly 6 years in prison   [22:28] on charges of money laundering and obstruction  of justice. He was later sentenced to an   [22:33] additional two years for ANOTHER theft  of Bitcoin from the U.S. government. [22:39] Thanks for watching. For Newsthink, I’m Cindy Pom.
